retrieved_docs.title: Periampullary Tumors -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Martin proposed a 4-stage system. Stage I - Vegetating tumor limited to the epithelium with no involvement of the sphincter of Oddi Stage II - Tumor localized in the duodenal submucosa without the involvement of the duodenal muscularis propria but possible involvement of the sphincter of Oddi Stage III - Tumor of the duodenal muscularis propria Stage IV - Tumor of the periduodenal area or pancreas, with proximal or distal lymph node involvement

retrieved_docs.title: Periampullary Tumors -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Ampullary cancer arises from ampulla of Vater terminal to the confluence of the distal common bile duct (CBD) and the pancreatic duct. It is important to distinguish ampullary carcinoma from periampullary tumors such as extra-biliary or pancreatic tumors due to wide variation in clinical outcomes and therapeutic management. For instance, pancreatic cancers and biliary tract tumors such as cholangiocarcinoma are associated with significantly worse prognosis with limited treatment options compared to ampullary cancers, even in early disease presentations.  Recent studies further evaluated histological variation depending on the epithelium of origin. Ampullary carcinoma is commonly intestinal epithelial at about 47 percent or pancreato-biliary epithelial subtypes at about 24 percent.

retrieved_docs.title: Periampullary Tumors -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical presentation of ampullary cancers often resembles the presentation of distal biliary ductal cancers (cholangiocarcinomas), pancreatic cancers, or colon cancers. Often the FNAC is difficult to differentiate ampullary from other periampullary cancers. Definitive surgery allows prospectively identifying histology accurately. From the biologic behavior standpoint, ampullary cancers are associated with a better prognosis compared to other periampullary malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: Periampullary Tumors -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognostic outcome is dependent upon the extent of local invitation, the status of surgical margins after the definitive surgery, and the presence of nodal metastasis. Node positivity is a key factor in determining the overall prognosis. The five-year survival rate range from 70% to 80% for node-negative disease compared to 20% to 50% for node-positive disease. [12] Recently the role of histology phenotype has emerged with some data indicating poor prognosis associated with pancreaticobiliary histopathology compared to intestinal type.

retrieved_docs.title: Periampullary Tumors -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The histology of ampullary cancers commonly resembles that of adenocarcinomas of intestinal origin rather than pancreaticobiliary origin. [3] Likewise, the biologic behavior of these tumors often resembles intestinal than the pancreaticobiliary phenotype. The histology is differentiated by the expression of cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) by ampullary cancers that points towards intestinal origin than pancreato-biliary origin. [4] The later is associated with a worse prognosis. [1] These cancers are thought to arise from premalignant precursor lesions-ampullary adenomas. These tumors commonly associated with K-ras mutations. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Periampullary Tumors -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The TNM system of the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) is used to stage these cancers. The diagnosis is established with endoscopic ultrasound / Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) and fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC). If a patient presents with obstructive jaundice, these modalities could allow therapeutic intervention will be done at the time of diagnostic evaluation with biliary stent placement. As most of the patients undergo surgery, temporary plastic stents are preferred to metallic stents. All patients diagnosed with ampullary cancers need staging evaluation with computerized tomography (CT) of chest, abdomen, and pelvis. Often clear differentiation of primary ampullary cancers from other periampullary malignancies such as those arising in the pancreas, biliary duct, or intestine is challenging at the time of diagnostic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: Periampullary Tumors -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Ampullary cancer arises from ampulla of Vater terminal to the confluence of the distal common bile duct (CBD) and the pancreatic duct. It is important to distinguish ampullary carcinoma from periampullary tumors such as extra-biliary or pancreatic tumors due to wide variation in clinical outcomes and therapeutic management. For instance, pancreatic cancers and biliary tract tumors such as cholangiocarcinoma are associated with significantly worse prognosis with limited treatment options compared to ampullary cancers, even in early disease presentations. Recent studies further evaluated histological variation depending on the epithelium of origin. Ampullary carcinoma is commonly intestinal epithelial (about 47 percent) or pancreato-biliary (about 24 percent) epithelial subtypes. This activity outlines the ampullary cancer disease management. The role of chemotherapy and radiation in the management of ampullary cancer by an interprofessional team will be discussed.

retrieved_docs.title: Cholangiocarcinoma -- Surgical Oncology -- Perihilar Cholangiocarcinoma
retrieved_docs.content: Operative decision-making in pCCA is complex, as identifying the extent of the tumor may be difficult. Even with high-quality imaging and endoscopic techniques, the proximal and distal extent of the tumor may not be visualized preoperatively and only be identified intraoperatively. The extrahepatic bile duct and the involved hemiliver are usually resected with a portal lymphadenectomy. Routine resection of the caudate lobe is also recommended, given its proximity to the biliary confluence and its involvement in many tumors. To ensure negative margins, frozen section margins on the bile duct margins should be performed before reconstruction. The Bismuth-Corlette classification is commonly used for perihilar tumors and guides surgical management (see Image. Bismuth Corlette Classification of Perihilar Cholangiocarcinoma). [44] [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Periampullary Tumors -- Radiation Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation is an option for the selected subset of patients who are at high risk for recurrences such as positive or close surgical margin or positive nodal disease. However, the definitive evidence on the overall clinical benefit lacks due to the relative rarity of this disease and the lack of large randomized studies. [10] The commonly used dose is 50.4 grays given in 4 to 6 weeks concurrently with fluoropyrimidine or gemcitabine-based treatment in adjuvant setting either sandwiched between chemotherapy or after 4 to 5 months of chemotherapy. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Cholangiocarcinoma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cholangiocarcinoma is an aggressive malignancy of biliary epithelium that may arise anywhere in the biliary tract, from the intrahepatic biliary canaliculi to the terminus where the common bile duct enters the duodenum at the duodenal ampulla. Cholangiocarcinoma is classified by anatomical origin as intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA) or extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (eCCA); eCCA is subdivided into perihilar cholangiocarcinoma (pCCA) and distal cholangiocarcinoma (dCCA). More than 95% of cholangiocarcinomas are adenocarcinomas.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Tract Cancer(Archived) -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Traditionally, cholangiocarcinoma was histologically classified as poorly differentiated or well-differentiated adenocarcinoma: gland forming or tubular, squamous, mucinous, signet cells, and some other rare variants of the tumor. The American Joint Committee for Cancer (AJCC) modified this classification from simply well-differentiated to poorly differentiated carcinoma on the histological basis to: Adenocarcinoma Intraductal papillary neoplasm without any invasive characteristics Mucinous cystic neoplasm with an associated invasive carcinoma Adenosquamous carcinoma Squamous cell carcinoma Poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma Mixed adenoneuroendocrine carcinoma and undifferentiated carcinoma. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Tract Cancer(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The biliary tract comprises of gallbladder and intra and extrahepatic biliary tree. Bile is directed through these ducts to the second part of duodenum at major duodenal papilla. The epithelium of the biliary tract is lined with cells called cholangiocytes. Carcinoma of the biliary tract arises from the malignant transformation of the epithelium of the bile ducts which is made up of these cholangiocytes, and is categorized on the basis of its anatomical location as; 1) Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma 2) Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, which includes; perihilar tumor also known as Klatskin tumor (originating from the epithelium of the bile duct at the junction of right and left hepatic ducts with the cystic duct where it forms the common bile duct) and distal cholangiocarcinoma outspreading to encompass the gallbladder, ampulla of Vater and pancreatic biliary ducts. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatobiliary Tract Cancer(Archived) -- Surgical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Surgery is the only curative treatment for selected patients. The surgical procedure differs according to the anatomical location. Distal lesions are treated with pancreaticoduodenectomy, the Whipple procedure. Intrahepatic lesions will require hepatectomy, and lymphadenectomy is rarely performed. For hilar lesions, surgical resection will vary according to the Bismuth-Corlette anatomical classification.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatobiliary Tract Cancer(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Surgery is the only curative treatment for selected patients. The surgical procedure differs according to the anatomical location. Distal lesions are treated with pancreaticoduodenectomy, the Whipple procedure. Intrahepatic lesions will require hepatectomy, and lymphadenectomy is rarely performed. For hilar lesions, surgical resection will vary according to the Bismuth-Corlette anatomical classification.

retrieved_docs.title: Periampullary Tumors -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Ampullary cancer patients with T2 or above stage or lymph node-positive tumors are associated with increased recurrence risk and therefore, could be benefitted from adjuvant treatment. A common strategy in the United States is adjuvant chemotherapy with gemcitabine or fluoropyrimidine for 6 months if systemic therapy alone is chosen. Patients presented with large tumors, positive margins, and node positivity are currently being treated with adding 6 weeks of concurrent chemoradiation eight upfront or the end of systemic chemotherapy. The American Joint Committee on Cancer staging system uses the TNM system.

retrieved_docs.title: Periampullary Tumors -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of ampullary cancer. Outline the diagnostic workup of ampullary cancers. Review the management options available for ampullary cancers. Describe interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance existing care and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: carcinomas under the broader category of primary malignant neoplasms. Collectively, these relationships delineate a structured view of digestive system cancers, especially pancreatic malignancies, their metastatic characteristics, and their placement within a comprehensive neoplasm classification system that spans multiple organ sites and includes both well‑defined and uncertain neoplastic entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2555
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of head of pancreas represents a specific morphological form of الورم السرطاني, indicating that this pancreatic head malignancy is a particular type of cancerous tumor. Recognizing this association clarifies that the carcinoma located in the head of the pancreas falls under the broader category of malignant neoplasms, which is essential for accurate pathological classification, diagnosis, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 211
retrieved_docs.content: are each classified as cancerous tumors and are included within the general categories of pancreatic cancer and malignant neoplasms, respectively. Collectively, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical organization in which specific gastrointestinal, pancreatic, head‑and‑neck, sublingual‑gland, and cutaneous malignancies are linked to broader concepts of secondary malignancies and non‑hematologic malignant disease groups. This structure supports consistent clinical coding, precise morphological description, and comprehensive patient management across diverse cancer types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1107
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is associated with pancreatic cancer, indicating that this genetic or clinical syndrome maps to a malignant neoplasm of the pancreas. Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas is described as an “الورم السرطاني” (cancerous tumor) in its associated morphology and is also mapped to pancreatic cancer, identifying it as a specific histologic subtype located in the pancreatic head. Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas similarly maps to pancreatic cancer, representing another terminology for a malignant tumor arising in the same region. Together, Bard‑Pic syndrome, carcinoma of the head of the pancreas, and primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas all refer to malignant pancreatic disease, with the latter two emphasizing the anatomical location (head of the pancreas) and morphological characteristics of the tumor, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, classification, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 901
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of head of pancreas is identified as a form of الورم السرطاني, indicating that this pancreatic cancer represents a malignant tumor morphology. Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is also classified under الورم السرطاني, confirming its status as a malignant tumor, and it is further associated with the morphology of السرطان النقيلي, emphasizing that this disease involves metastatic cancer spread to lymph nodes in the head and neck region. Together, these relationships illustrate that both the pancreatic head carcinoma and the metastatic head‑and‑neck lymph‑node carcinoma are subsets of malignant tumors, with the latter specifically exemplifying metastatic cancer, a distinction that is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1243
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of head of pancreas and Primary malignant neoplasm of head of pancreas are both identified as cancer of pancreas, indicating that each term refers to a malignant tumor arising in the head region of the pancreas.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1131
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of head of pancreas is mapped to the broader term cancer of pancreas, and cancer of pancreas is classified as سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس. This relationship indicates that the specific malignant tumor located in the head of the pancreas corresponds to pancreatic cancer and is considered a form of metastatic cancer of the pancreatic head, providing a clear hierarchical classification for clinical documentation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 871
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of head of pancreas is linked to the morphology of ورم خبيث أولي, indicating that this pancreatic cancer represents a specific form of a primary malignant tumor. In addition, سرطانة توتية is classified as a subtype of ورم خبيث أولي, showing that it also falls under the broader category of primary malignant tumors. Together, these relationships highlight that both the pancreatic head neoplasm and سرطانة توتية are particular manifestations within the overarching concept of ورم خبيث أولي.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 156
retrieved_docs.content: the pancreatic cancer hierarchy, “cancer of pancreas” is defined as an ISA relationship to سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس (metastatic cancer in the head of the pancreas), reinforcing its classification as a metastatic pancreatic malignancy. Finally, the classification system associates سرطان العظم (bone cancer) with ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي (malignant tumor of the upper jaw), illustrating how bone malignancies are categorized under specific anatomical malignant tumor descriptors. Collectively, these relationships integrate multilingual terminology, hierarchical disease classifications, and morphological mappings to provide a coherent framework for understanding gastrointestinal and related neoplastic disorders across different linguistic and clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1208
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of head of pancreas is identified as a specific form of pancreatic cancer and is associated with the broader morphological category of الورم السرطاني (malignant tumor). This relationship indicates that the carcinoma of the pancreatic head is a subtype of malignant tumor within the pancreas. Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is likewise linked to الورم السرطاني through an inverse‑isa relationship, establishing it as a particular instance of a malignant tumor. In addition, metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is associated with the morphology of السرطان النقيلي (metastatic cancer), highlighting its nature as a cancer that has spread to regional lymph nodes. Together, these connections illustrate how specific carcinoma entities—Carcinoma of head of pancreas and Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck—fit within the general concepts of malignant tumor (الورم السرطاني) and, in the case of the metastatic disease, the specific category of metastatic cancer (السرطان النقيلي), providing a clear framework for understanding their classification and pathological relationships.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2426
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas is a specific malignant tumor located in the pancreatic head that is classified under the broader category of pancreatic cancer, indicating that it maps directly to cancer of the pancreas.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 235
retrieved_docs.content: frequently co‑occurs with renal tumors such as الورم الأورمي الكلوي (oncocytoma) and سرطان الخلايا الكلوية (renal cell carcinoma), linking diagnostic terminology with appropriate oncologic surgery. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how malignant cellular infiltrates, metastatic and secondary lesions—particularly in supraclavicular lymph nodes—serve as critical diagnostic markers for thymic carcinoma, pancreatic cancer, renal and ovarian cancers, and bone malignancies, and how multilingual nomenclature and procedural associations support accurate staging, prognosis, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2656
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of head of pancreas is a malignant neoplasm located in the head region of the pancreas and is classified as a type of cancer of the pancreas. It is characterized by the morphological features of الورم السرطاني, the Arabic term for a cancerous tumor, indicating its aggressive and invasive nature. This specific pancreatic carcinoma is therefore mapped to the broader category of pancreatic cancer, highlighting its role as a distinct yet related entity within pancreatic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1905
retrieved_docs.content: Benign tumor cells are not regarded as a finding of pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي), indicating that their presence does not support a diagnosis of this malignancy. In contrast, tumor cells whose nature is uncertain—whether benign or malignant—are considered abnormal cells of pancreatic cancer, reflecting their potential relevance to the disease process. Recognizing the difference between clearly benign cells and cells of indeterminate behavior is essential for accurate pathological assessment and appropriate patient management in the context of pancreatic cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1033
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of head of pancreas is identified as a malignant tumor (الورم السرطاني) and is directly mapped to the broader term cancer of pancreas. Cancer of pancreas is classified as a metastatic cancer located in the head of the pancreas (سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس). Likewise, metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is described as a malignant tumor (الورم السرطاني) whose associated morphology is that of metastatic cancer (السرطان النقيلي). These interconnections show that both the primary pancreatic carcinoma in the head and the metastatic carcinoma involving head‑and‑neck lymph nodes represent forms of malignant, often metastatic, neoplasms, underscoring the need to recognize their shared pathology for accurate diagnosis and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of head of pancreas and primary malignant neoplasm of head of pancreas are both recognized as forms of cancer of the pancreas, specifically malignant neoplasms arising in the head region of the organ. Both entities are linked to the Arabic term الورم السرطاني, which denotes a cancerous tumor morphology, underscoring their nature as aggressive, malignant growths. Consequently, these terms are used interchangeably in medical contexts to describe pancreatic head cancers, highlighting their shared classification as malignant pancreatic tumors.
